analogy: Think of "Introducing Western Sociologists" through the chapter's own opening images: 65 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS CHAPTER 4 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS Sociology is sometimes called the child of the ‘age of revolution’. This is because it was born in 19th century Western Europe, after revolutionary changes in the preceding three centuries that decisively changed the way people... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 65 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS, CHAPTER 4, INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS, THE CONTEXT OF SOCIOLOGY. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Introducing Western Sociologists is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 65 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS CHAPTER 4 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS Sociology is sometimes called the child of the ‘age of revolution’.

The text explains that this is because it was born in 19th century Western Europe, after revolutionary changes in the preceding three centuries that decisively changed the way people lived.

The text explains that three revolutions paved the way for the emergence of sociology: the Enlightenment, or the scientific revolution; the French Revolution; and the Industrial Revolution.

The text explains that these processes completely transformed not only European society, but also the rest of the world as it came into contact with Europe.

The text explains that in this chapter the key ideas of three sociological thinkers: Karl Marx, Emile Durkheim and Max Weber will be discussed.

The text explains that as part of the classical tradition of sociology, they laid the foundation of the subject.

The text explains that their ideas and insights have remained relevant even in the contemporary period.

The text explains that of course, these ideas have also been subjected to criticism and have undergone major modifications.

The text explains that but since ideas a b o u t s o c i e t y a re t h e m s e l v e s influenced by social conditions, we begin with a few words about the context in which sociology emerged.

The text explains that tHE CONTEXT OF SOCIOLOGY The modern era in Europe and the conditions of modernity that we take for granted today were brought about by three major processes.

The text explains that these were: the Enlightenment or dawning of the ‘age of reason’; the quest for political sovereignty embodied in the French Revolution; and the system of mass manufacture inaugurated by the Industrial Revolution.

The text explains that since these have been discussed at length in Chapter 1 of Introducing Sociology, here we will only mention some of the intellectual consequences of these momentous changes.

The text explains that what sorts of changes were these three processes associated with?

The text explains that 66 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY The Enlightenment During the late 17th and 18th centuries, Western Europe saw the emergence of radically new ways of thinking about the world.

The text explains that refered to as ‘The Enlightenment’, these new philosophies established the human being at the centre of the universe, and rational thought as the central feature of the human being.

The text explains that the ability to think rationally and critically transformed the individual human being into both the producer and the user of all knowledge, the ‘knowing subject’.

The text explains that on the other hand, only persons who could think and reason could be considered as fully human.

The text explains that being the handiwork of humans, society was amenable to rational analysis and thus comprehensible to other humans.

The text explains that for reason to become the defining feature of the human world, it was necessary to displace nature, religion and the divine acts of gods from the central position they had in earlier ways of understanding the world.

The text explains that the Declaration of Human Rights asserted the equality of all citizens and questioned the legitimacy of privileges inherited by birth.

The text explains that as free citizens of the republic, sovereign individuals were invested with rights and were equal before the law and other institutions of the state.

The text explains that moreover, the nation-state itself was also redefined as a sovereign entity with a centralised government.

The text explains that the first was the systematic application of science and technology to industrial production, particularly the invention of new machines and the harnessing of new sources of power.

The text explains that new machines like the Spinning Jenny (which greatly increased the productivity of the textile industry) and new methods of obtaining power (such as the various versions of the steam engine) facilitated the production process and gave rise to the factory system and mass manufacture of goods.

The text explains that these changes in the production system also resulted in major changes in social life.

The text explains that from the outset sociological thought was concerned with the scientific analysis of developments in industrial society.

The text explains that e m p i r i c a l l y i n f o r m e d scientific discussion about trends in s o c i a l b e h a v i o u r o n l y b e c a m e possible with the advent of modern industrial society.

The text explains that the scientific information generated by the state to monitor and maintain the health of its social body became the basis for reflection on society.

The text explains that 68 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY he engaged in a critical analysis of capitalist society to expose its weaknesses and bring about its downfall.

The text explains that marx argued that human society had progressed through different stages.

The text explains that 1859: A Contribution to the Critique of Political Economy (published).

The text explains that 69 INTRODUCING WESTERN SOCIOLOGISTS Capitalist society was marked by an ever intensifying process of alienation operating at several levels.

The text explains that first, modern capitalist society is one where humans are more alienated from nature than ever before; second, human beings are alienated from each other as capitalism individualises previously collective forms of social organisation, and as relationships get more and more market-mediated.

The text explains that moreover, workers have no control over the work process itself — unlike in the days when skilled craftsmen controlled their own labour, today the content of the factory worker’s working day is decided by the management.

The text explains that finally, as the combined result of all these alienations, human beings are also alienated from themselves and struggle to make their lives meaningful in a system where they are both more free but also more alienated and less in control of their lives than before.

The text explains that however, even though it was an exploitative and oppressive system, Marx believed that capitalism was nevertheless a necessary and progressive stage of human history because it created the preconditions for an egalitarian future free from both exploitation and poverty.

The text explains that capitalist society would be transformed by its victims, i.e. the working class, who would unite to collectively bring about a revolution to overthrow it and establish a free and equal socialist society.

The text explains that marx’s conception of the economy was based on the notion of a mode of production, which stood for a broad system of production associated with an epoch or historical period.

The text explains that productive forces refer to all the means or factors of production such as land, labour, technology, sources of energy (such as electricity, coal, petroleum and so on).

The text explains that 70 UNDERSTANDING SOCIETY like simple stone tools and hunting weapons.

The text explains that on this base rested all the social, cultural and political institutions of society.

The text explains that thus, institutions like religion, art, law, literature or different forms of beliefs and ideas were all part of the ‘superstructure’ which was built on top of the base.

The text explains that marx argued that people’s ideas and beliefs originated from the economic system of which they were part.

The text explains that what are the various components of a mode of production?

The text explains that why do classes come into conflict, according to Marx?

The text explains that what is the difference between ‘mechanical’ and ‘organic’ solidarity?

The text explains that show, with examples, how moral codes are indicators of social solidarity.

The text explains that what is special or different about the kind of objectivity needed in social science?

The text explains that can you identify any ideas or theories which have led to the formation of social movements in India in recent times?

The text explains that try to find out what Marx and Weber wrote about India.

The text explains that can you think of reasons why we should study the work of thinkers who died long ago?

The text explains that what could be some reasons to not study them?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society, Chapter 4: Introducing Western Sociologists

This is because it was born in 19th century Western Europe, after revolutionary changes in the preceding three centuries that decisively changed the way people lived. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 1)

Three revolutions paved the way for the emergence of sociology: the Enlightenment, or the scientific revolution; the French Revolution; and the Industrial Revolution. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 1)
These processes completely transformed not only European society, but also the rest of the world as it came into contact with Europe. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 1)
In this chapter the key ideas of three sociological thinkers: Karl Marx, Emile Durkheim and Max Weber will be discussed. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 1)
As part of the classical tradition of sociology, they laid the foundation of the subject. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 1)
Their ideas and insights have remained relevant even in the contemporary period. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 1)
Of course, these ideas have also been subjected to criticism and have undergone major modifications. (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 1)
But since ideas a b o u t s o c i e t y a re t h e m s e l v e... (NCERT Class 11 Understanding Society Ch4 pg 1)
